content:
A feature store is a centralised repository for computed, versioned ML features. The pitch sounds simple: compute a feature once, reuse it everywhere. In practice, most teams reach for a feature store at the wrong time — before they have enough models in production to justify the operational overhead.

## When you actually need one

You probably need a feature store when: (a) you have multiple models consuming the same features and are tired of copying transformation logic across notebooks; (b) you need point-in-time correct joins to prevent training–serving skew; (c) you have a streaming data source and need features available at sub-second latency.

You probably do not need one when you have fewer than five models in production, when your training cadence is weekly or slower, or when all your features are batch-computed from a warehouse you already trust.

## The training–serving skew problem

The most dangerous thing a feature store solves — and the thing most teams learn about the hard way — is training–serving skew. This happens when the code that computes a feature at training time differs from the code that computes it at inference time. Even a tiny difference (timezone handling, null imputation, rounding) can tank production performance while offline metrics look fine.

A well-implemented feature store enforces a single definition. You write the transformation once; the store applies it consistently at both training and serving time.

## The simpler alternative

For many teams, the right answer is a versioned Parquet file on S3 combined with strict naming conventions and a README. It is boring, it works, and it does not require on-call rotation for a new piece of infrastructure. Start here. Graduate when the pain is real.

Imagine your fraud detection model outputs a score of 0.9 for a transaction. Does that mean there is a 90 % probability the transaction is fraudulent? For most models, the answer is: definitely not, and probably not even close.

Calibration is the property of a model where the output score matches the empirical probability of the event. A perfectly calibrated model that outputs 0.9 for 100 transactions should see approximately 90 of them be positive.

## Why models are miscalibrated by default

Most training objectives do not optimise for calibration. Cross-entropy loss pushes logits toward ± ∞ rather than toward the data's base rate. Tree-based methods (gradient boosting, random forests) produce scores that cluster near 0 and 1 because they are aggregating binary splits. Neural networks are notoriously overconfident, particularly when evaluated on data slightly outside the training distribution.

## Measuring calibration

The reliability diagram (also called a calibration curve) is the most intuitive tool. Bin your predictions into deciles, plot predicted probability on the x-axis against observed positive rate on the y-axis. A perfectly calibrated model lies on the diagonal. Overconfident models bow above it; underconfident models dip below.

The Expected Calibration Error (ECE) summarises this as a single number: the weighted average of the absolute difference between predicted probability and observed accuracy across bins.

## Fixing it

Platt scaling and isotonic regression are the two standard post-hoc recalibration methods. Platt scaling fits a logistic regression on top of your model's scores using a held-out calibration set. Isotonic regression is more flexible but requires more data to avoid overfitting. Temperature scaling is a single-parameter version of Platt scaling that works particularly well for neural networks.

Calibration is worth measuring on every model you deploy. It costs almost nothing, and it tells you whether you can trust your own confidence scores.

The single biggest reliability problem in production ML is not model drift — it is untested, undocumented data transformation code scattered across notebooks, Airflow tasks, and bespoke Python scripts that nobody fully understands.

dbt (data build tool) was designed to solve exactly this for analytics, but it applies equally well to ML feature pipelines.

## What dbt gives you

**Version control.** Every transformation is a SQL file in a git repo. You get blame, history, pull requests, and review.

**Tests.** dbt lets you declare expectations (not null, unique, accepted values, referential integrity) that run on every build. You catch bad data before it reaches your model.

**Documentation.** Column-level descriptions live in YAML alongside the model. dbt generates a browsable data catalogue automatically.

**Lineage.** dbt builds a DAG of your transformations. You can see, visually, how raw events become the feature that feeds your model.

## The pattern I use

I organise my dbt project into three layers: `staging` (light cleaning and renaming of raw sources), `intermediate` (business logic, joins, aggregations), and `features` (point-in-time snapshots ready for training). The `features` layer models are parameterised by `as_of_date` so I can generate historical slices for backtesting.

Training jobs read directly from the `features` layer. Serving infrastructure calls the same dbt-compiled SQL via a thin API. Training–serving skew is eliminated by construction.

## Caveats

This approach works cleanly for batch features. If you need low-latency online features (sub-100 ms), you will need something else — a Redis cache, a dedicated feature store, or a streaming pipeline. dbt is a batch tool, and it should not pretend otherwise.

Every few months, a new model tops the MMLU leaderboard. Researchers celebrate, press releases go out, and then practitioners try to deploy the model and find it hallucinates on the exact use case they care about.

This is the LLM evaluation gap: the distance between what benchmarks measure and what production applications need.

## Why benchmarks plateau and fail

Benchmarks become contaminated. MMLU questions appear in web crawls, which appear in training data. Models do not generalise — they partially memorise. This is not malfeasance; it is an almost unavoidable consequence of training on internet-scale data.

Benchmarks measure narrow things. Multiple choice is not generation. Aggregate accuracy conceals variance. A model that scores 85 % on MMLU might score 85 % because it aces history and fails biology, or because it averages across both. The aggregate number tells you almost nothing about behaviour on your specific task.

Benchmarks don't model distribution shift. Your production data is different from benchmark data in ways that matter: domain vocabulary, user phrasing, the long tail of edge cases. A model fine-tuned on benchmark-adjacent data will look better on evals and worse in deployment.

## What actually works

Task-specific golden sets outperform general benchmarks for production decisions. Build a dataset of 200–500 examples drawn from your actual use case, with human-labelled ground truth. Evaluate every candidate model on it. Refresh the golden set quarterly as your use case evolves.

Human evaluation at the margin matters. When two models are close on automated metrics, put them in front of real users and measure preference. Automated metrics are proxies; user preference is the thing you actually care about.

Failure mode analysis is underrated. Pick 50 examples where your current model fails. Categorise them. Build your evaluation suite around those failure modes. A benchmark you construct from your own failure cases will tell you more than any general benchmark about whether a new model is actually better.
